items.q: What changed between the MDGs (Millennium Development Goals) and the SDGs (Sustainable Development Goals)?
items.correct: MDGs (2000-2015) targeted LICs on basic poverty/health/education; SDGs (2015-2030) apply to all countries and include environmental and inequality goals — broader, more universal scope.
items.misconception: The SDGs replaced the MDGs because the MDGs were achieved.
items.random_wrong: MDGs and SDGs are the same — just renamed.
items.why: Most MDGs were partly achieved but with major gaps. SDGs expanded scope to include HICs (which all face sustainability challenges) and added climate, inequality and oceans — reflecting a shift in development thinking.

items.q: Why do earthquakes of similar magnitude cause different amounts of damage?
items.correct: Damage depends on depth, distance from population, building quality, ground type (liquefaction risk), time of day, and emergency preparedness — not just magnitude.
items.misconception: Bigger magnitude always means more deaths.
items.random_wrong: All earthquakes of magnitude 7 cause the same damage everywhere.
items.why: Magnitude measures energy; impact depends on context. A magnitude 7 in a poor, dense city kills thousands; the same offshore or in a wealthy area may kill no one — a key development comparison.

items.q: What drives the movement of tectonic plates?
items.correct: Convection currents in the mantle combined with slab pull at subduction zones and ridge push at constructive boundaries.
items.misconception: The Earth's rotation makes the plates move.
items.random_wrong: Plates move because of ocean tides pulling on the crust.
items.why: Students conflate the Earth spinning with crustal motion. Plate motion is driven by heat-driven convection in the mantle, not rotation — rotation only affects things like the Coriolis effect.
Correct answer: Hess. “Wegener” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was the Tohoku earthquake magnitude?
items.why: It's 9.0. “7.0” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How tall (m) was the Tohoku tsunami?
items.why: The real figure is 15 m. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
